subject: Mathematics
q: If one of the diameters of the circle, given by the equation, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is a chord of a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math>, whose centre is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is
question: If one of the diameters of the circle, given by the equation, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is a chord of a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math>, whose centre is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is
solution:
The centre of the given circle x2+y2-4x+6y-12=0 is 2, -3 and the radius is 5.

​​​​​​​

The distance between the centres 52 and radius is 5. The triangle OPQ is a right angled triangle

OQ= 522+52= 532=53

Hence answer is 53

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $C$ be the circle of minimum area touching the parabola $y=6-x^2$ and the lines $y=\sqrt{3}|x|$. Then, which one of the following points lies on the circle $C$ ?
question: Let $C$ be the circle of minimum area touching the parabola $y=6-x^2$ and the lines $y=\sqrt{3}|x|$. Then, which one of the following points lies on the circle $C$ ?
solution: Equation of circle $\mathrm{x}^2+(\mathrm{y}-(6-\mathrm{r}))^2=\mathrm{r}^2$ touches $\sqrt{3} \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}=0$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{p}=\mathrm{r} \\ & \frac{|0-(6-\mathrm{r})|}{2}=\mathrm{r} \\ & |\mathrm{r}-6|=2 \mathrm{r} \\ & \mathrm{r}=2 \end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ Circle $\mathrm{x}^2+(\mathrm{y}-4)^2=4$ $(2,4)$ Satisfies this equation

subject: Mathematics
q: Two tangents are drawn from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, such that the angle between these tangents is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>12</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>12</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>∈</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math> If the centre of the circle is denoted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and these tangents touch the circle at points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, then the ratio of the areas of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is :
question: Two tangents are drawn from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, such that the angle between these tangents is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>12</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>12</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>∈</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math> If the centre of the circle is denoted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and these tangents touch the circle at points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, then the ratio of the areas of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is :
solution:
Let, the angle between the tangents PA and PB from the point P to the circle x2+y2-2x-4y+4=0 with centre C is θ.

Given θ=tan-1125  ⇒tanθ=125.

We know that the centre and radius of a circle x2+y2+2gx+2fy+c=0 are respectively -g, -f and g2+f2-c.

Thus, the centre and radius of the circle x2+y2-2x-4y+4=0 are respectively 1, 2 and 12+22-4=1 unit.

We know that the line joining the centre of a circle to the point of contact of the tangent is perpendicular to the tangent, hence ∠CAP=∠CBP=90° also CP bisects the angle ∠APB as ∆CAP≅∆CBP by SSS congruency.

Thus, we have ∠CPA=∠CPB=θ2

In ∆CAP, we have tanθ2=ACAP

⇒tanθ2=1AP

⇒AP=cotθ2

Now, area of ΔPAB=12PAPBsinθ

Since, tangents to a circle from an external point are equal, hence PA=PB

Area of ΔPAB=12PA2sinθ

=12cot2θ2sinθ

=12cos2θ2sin2θ2sinθ

Using cos2x=2cos2x-1=1-2sin2x, we get

Area of ΔPAB=121+cosθ1-cosθsinθ

We have tanθ=125 ⇒sinθ=1213 & cosθ=513

Area of ΔPAB=121+5131-5131213

=12×188×1213=2726 sq units.

And, area of ΔCAB=12ACBCsinπ-θ

=12sinθ=121213=613 sq units.

∴  area of ΔPAB area of ΔCAB=2726613=94.

subject: Mathematics
q: Locus of mid point of the portion between the axes of $x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p$ where $p$ is constant is
question: Locus of mid point of the portion between the axes of $x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p$ where $p$ is constant is
solution: Equation of $A B$ is $x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p \Rightarrow \frac{x \cos \alpha}{p}+\frac{y \sin \alpha}{p}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{p / \cos \alpha}+\frac{y}{p / \sin \alpha}=1$ So co-ordinates of $A$ and $B$ are $\left(\frac{p}{\cos \alpha}, 0\right)$ and $\left(0, \frac{p}{\sin \alpha}\right)$; So coordinates of mid point of $A B$ are $\left(\frac{p}{2 \cos \alpha}, \frac{p}{2 \sin \alpha}\right)=\left(x_1, y_1\right)($ let $) ; x_1=\frac{p}{2 \cos \alpha} \& y_1=\frac{p}{2 \sin \alpha}$ $\Rightarrow \cos \alpha=p / 2 x_1$ and $\sin \alpha=p / 2 y_1 ; \cos ^2 \alpha+\sin ^2 \alpha=1 \Rightarrow \frac{p^2}{4}\left(\frac{1}{x_1^2}+\frac{1}{y_1^2}\right)=1$ Locus of $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ is $\frac{1}{x^2}+\frac{1}{y^2}=\frac{4}{p^2}$.

subject: Mathematics
q: If a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> passes through the origin <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> and intersects the coordinate axes at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, then the locus of the foot of perpendicular from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is :
question: If a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> passes through the origin <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> and intersects the coordinate axes at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, then the locus of the foot of perpendicular from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is :
solution:
Since, circle passing through origin intersect the coordinate axes at A& B, hence AB must be diameter and AB=2R.

Now, let foot of the perpendicular from origin upon AB be Ph, k.

Slope of line OP=k-0h-0=kh

Since, line AB⊥OP⇒ slope of AB=-hk

Thus, equation of line  AB is y-k=-hkx-h

For co-ordinates of A, put y=0⇒0-k=-hkx-h⇒x=h2+k2h⇒A h2+k2h, 0.

For co-ordinates of B, put x=0⇒y-k=-hk0-h⇒y=h2+k2k⇒B 0,h2+k2k.

Now, given AB=2R

Applying distance formula,

⇒h2+k2h-02+0-h2+k2k2=2R

⇒h2+k22 1h2+1k2=4R2⇒h2+k23=4R2h2k2

Hence, locus is x2+y23=4R2x2y2

subject: Mathematics
q: If the common tangents to the parabola, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi></math> and the circle, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> intersect at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, then the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> from the origin (units), is:
question: If the common tangents to the parabola, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi></math> and the circle, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> intersect at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, then the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> from the origin (units), is:
solution:
Let y=mx+c be the common tangent.

Then c2=41+m2 ...(1) (condition of tangency for circle)

Solving with x2=4y, we get x2=4mx+c

i.e. x2-4mx-4c=0 ...(1)

Being a tangent, (1) must have equal roots.

⇒-4m2=41-4c⇒m2=-c ...(2)

From 1 & 2, c2=4-4c & c<0

⇒c2+4c-4=0⇒c=-22-2 (As c<0, so c≠22-2).

So, both tangents have common y intercept and thus intersect at P0,-2-2.

Thus, distance of P from origin is 22+1 units.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the tangents drawn at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> on the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> intersect at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is equal to
question: If the tangents drawn at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> on the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> intersect at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Tangent at Rx1,y1 when point R is on the circle x2+y2+2gx+2fy+c=0 can be written as 

xx1+yy1+gx+x1+fy+y1+c=0.

Tangent at O will be 

-x+0-2y+0=0 ⇒x+2y=0

Tangent at P will be 

x1+5+2y-x+1+5-2y+2=0

⇒5x=5+5⇒x=5+1 and y=-5+12

So Q5+1,-5+12

Length of tangent L=OQ=6+25+3+52=52+522=5+52

We know area of triangle OPQ =RL3R2+L2

Here, radius of circle R=5

So area=5×5+5235+5+522

=52×125+75+755+5520+25+105+5=52200+80550+105

=5220+855+55-55-5=52100-205+405-4020=53+52=5+352

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the circles $C_1:(x-\alpha)^2+(y-\beta)^2=r_1^2$ and $C_2:(x-8)^2+\left(y-\frac{15}{2}\right)^2=r_2^2$ touch each other externally at the point $(6,6)$. If the point $(6,6)$ divides the line segment joining the centres of the circles $C_1$ and $C_2$ internally in the ratio $2: 1$, then $(\alpha+\beta)+4\left(r_1^2+r_2^2\right)$ equals
question: Let the circles $C_1:(x-\alpha)^2+(y-\beta)^2=r_1^2$ and $C_2:(x-8)^2+\left(y-\frac{15}{2}\right)^2=r_2^2$ touch each other externally at the point $(6,6)$. If the point $(6,6)$ divides the line segment joining the centres of the circles $C_1$ and $C_2$ internally in the ratio $2: 1$, then $(\alpha+\beta)+4\left(r_1^2+r_2^2\right)$ equals
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \because \frac{16+\alpha}{3}=6 \text { and } \frac{15+\beta}{3}=6 \\ & \Rightarrow(\alpha, \beta) \equiv(2,3)\end{aligned}$ Also, $\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{C}_2=\mathrm{r}_1+\mathrm{r}_2$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sqrt{(2-8)^2+\left(3-\frac{15}{2}\right)^2}=2 r_2+r_2 \\ & \Rightarrow r_2=\frac{5}{2} \Rightarrow r_1=2 r_2=5 \\ & \therefore(\alpha+\beta)+4\left(r_1^2+r_2^2\right) \\ & =5+4\left(\frac{25}{4}+25\right)=130\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> lie below the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. The line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> passes through the centre <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and intersects the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. The line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> touches <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. Then the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> from the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>51</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
question: Let a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> lie below the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. The line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> passes through the centre <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and intersects the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. The line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> touches <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. Then the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> from the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>51</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
solution:
Plotting the diagram of given data, we get

Finding intersection point of 4x+3y+2=0 and 3x-4y-11=0 we get 1,-2

Now finding the point P by parametric we get,

x-1cosθ=y+2sinθ=±5

y=-2+5-45=-6

x=1+535=4

Now finding distance of point P from line 5x-12y+51=0=54-12-6+5113

=20+72+5113

=14313=11

q: If a line <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math>, is a tangent to the circle <math><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, and it is perpendicular to a line <math><msub><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> where <math><msub><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is the tangent to the circle <math><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, then
question: If a line <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math>, is a tangent to the circle <math><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, and it is perpendicular to a line <math><msub><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> where <math><msub><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is the tangent to the circle <math><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, then
solution:
Slope of tangent to x2+y2=1 at 12,12
x2+y2=1
2x+2yy'=0
y'=-xy=-1
Given that, y=mx+c, is perpendicular to L1,
So, m=1 ⇒y=x+c

Now distance of 3,0 from y=x+c is
c+32=1
c2+6c+9=2
c2+6c+7=0.

q: The number of common tangents, to the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>18</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>131</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, is
question: The number of common tangents, to the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>18</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>131</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, is
solution:
If S≡x2+y2+2gx+2fy+c=0.

C≡-g,-f and r=g2+f2-c.

The given circles are x2+y2-18x-15y+131=0

⇒C19, 152, r1=81+2254-131=52

and x2+y2-6x-6y-7=0

⇒C23, 3, r2=9+9+7=5

⇒d=C1C2=9-32+152-32=36+814=152

⇒r1+r2=52+5=152

⇒C1C2=r1+r2

∴ Circles touch each other externally, 3 common tangents.

Hence this is the correct option.

q: A circle passes through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and touches the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Which one of the following equations can represent a diameter of this circle ?
question: A circle passes through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and touches the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Which one of the following equations can represent a diameter of this circle ?
solution:
As y-axis is tangent to the circle therefore the centre of the circle would be x,2 and distance of centre from 0,2 and -2,4 should be equal.

∴ h2+02=h+22+22

⇒h2=h2+4h+4+4

⇒h=-2

Hence, centre is -2,2.

∴ 2x-3y+10=0 is the diameter, as centre lies on it.

q: A line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> moves such that the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> remain on the periphery of a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. Then the locus of the point, that divides the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, is a circle of radius
question: A line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> moves such that the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> remain on the periphery of a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. Then the locus of the point, that divides the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, is a circle of radius
solution:
Given,

A line segment AB of length λ moves such that the points A and B remain on the periphery of a circle of radius λ,

Now taking the points on the circle of radius λ as Bλcosθ1,λsinθ1 & Aλcosθ2λsinθ2 and taking the point Ph,k which divides the line segment in AB of length λ in 2:3

Now plotting the diagram we get,

Now, let O be the origin and radius of circle is λ and  AB=λ and using distance formula we get,

AB=λ=λcosθ1-λcosθ22+λsinθ1-λsinθ22

⇒1=2-2 cosθ1-θ2

⇒cosθ1-θ2=12

Now using section formula we get,

h=2λ cosθ1+3λ cosθ25 and  k=2λ sinθ1+3λ sinθ25

Now squaring and adding above two value we get,

h2+k2=λ2254+9+12cosθ1-θ2

⇒h2+k2=λ225·19

Hence, Radius =λ519

subject: Mathematics
q: A square is inscribed in the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x+8 y-103=0$ with its sides parallel to the coordinate axes. Then the distance of the vertex of this square which is nearest to the origin is:
question: A square is inscribed in the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x+8 y-103=0$ with its sides parallel to the coordinate axes. Then the distance of the vertex of this square which is nearest to the origin is:
solution:
The equation of circle is, $x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x+8 y-103=0$ $\Rightarrow(x-3)^{2}+(y+4)^{2}=(8 \sqrt{2})^{2}$ $C(3,-4), r=8 \sqrt{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ Length of side of square $=\sqrt{2} r=16$
$\Rightarrow P(-5,4), Q(-5,-12)$ $R(11,-12), S(11,4)$ $\Rightarrow$ Required distance $=O P$ $=\sqrt{(-5-0)^{2}+(-4-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{25+16}=\sqrt{41}$

The area of this triangle (in square units) is:
solution:
Equation tangent at P3,  1
x⋅3+y⋅1=4
x-intercept of this tangent is 43.
Hence, area of Δ=12⋅43⋅1=23

q: If the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>g</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>ℝ</mi></math> passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and its centre lies on the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>c</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math>, then the length of intercept made by the circle on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is
question: If the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>g</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>ℝ</mi></math> passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and its centre lies on the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>c</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math>, then the length of intercept made by the circle on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is
solution:
Given circle x2+y2-2gx+6y-19c=0

Also given circle passes through 6,1

So, 12g+19c=43     ⋯1

And centre g,-3 lies on x-2cy=8,

So, g+6c=8      …2

On solving equation 1 and 2 we get, c=1 and g=2

Now equation of circle will be,  x2+y2-4x+6y-19=0

Now length of intercept on x-axis is given by =2g2-C=222+19=223

q: Consider a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If the circle touches the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math>, whose distance from the origin is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math> , then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to _______.
question: Consider a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If the circle touches the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math>, whose distance from the origin is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math> , then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to _______.
solution:
Given: x-α2+y-β2=50

The given equation of circle represent centre as α,β and radius as 52 units.

Now, x+y=0 is tangent to the given circle at P.

We know that, radius is perpendicular to tangent at the point of tangency.

⇒CP⊥x+y=0 and CP=r

⇒r=α×1+β×112+12

⇒52=α+β2

⇒50=α+β22

⇒α+β2=100

subject: Mathematics
q: If the points of intersection of the ellipses $x^{2}+2 y^{2}-6 x-12 y+23=0$ and $4 x^{2}+2 y^{2}-20 x-12 y+35=0$ lie on a circle of radius $r$ and centre $(a, b)$, then the value of $a b+18 r^{2}$ is
question: If the points of intersection of the ellipses $x^{2}+2 y^{2}-6 x-12 y+23=0$ and $4 x^{2}+2 y^{2}-20 x-12 y+35=0$ lie on a circle of radius $r$ and centre $(a, b)$, then the value of $a b+18 r^{2}$ is
solution:
The circle through intersection of the ellipses is $S_1 + \lambda S_2 = 0$.
$(1+4\lambda)x^2 + (2+2\lambda)y^2 - (6+20\lambda)x - (12+12\lambda)y + (23+35\lambda) = 0$.
For a circle: $1+4\lambda = 2+2\lambda \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{2}$.
Circle: $3x^2 + 3y^2 - 16x - 18y + \frac{81}{2} = 0 \Rightarrow x^2 + y^2 - \frac{16}{3}x - 6y + \frac{27}{2} = 0$.
Centre $(a,b) = \left(\frac{8}{3}, 3\right)$, $r^2 = \frac{64}{9} + 9 - \frac{27}{2} = \frac{47}{18}$.
$ab + 18r^2 = \frac{8}{3} \times 3 + 18 \times \frac{47}{18} = 8 + 47 = 55$.

q: If a circle passing through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> touches <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-intercept of the circle is
question: If a circle passing through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> touches <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-intercept of the circle is
solution:
Let a, 2 be the centre of the circle

Equation of the circle is

x-a2+y-22=a2 

Put  -1,0  in the equation of circle -1-a2+-22=a2

1+2a+a2+4=a2

2a=-5

a=-52

Equation of circle is

x+522+y-22=254

x intercept =2g2-c

=2254-4

=294

=3

q: If the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>  lies inside the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and the circle does not touch or intersect the coordinate axes, then the set of all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is the interval
question: If the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>  lies inside the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and the circle does not touch or intersect the coordinate axes, then the set of all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is the interval
solution:
x2+y2-6x-10y+p=0

 ∴ Centre is 3, 5 and radius is 34-p.

∵ 1, 4 lies inside the circle. 

So, 1+16-6-40+p<0

⇒p<29      ...1   

Circle neither touches nor cut the coordinate axes.
So, radius of circle 34-p<5 (for not touching x- axis ) and 34-p<3 (for not touching y-axis)

⇒ 9<p and 25<p 

⇒ p>25   ...2

From equations 1 & 2,

So,  p∈25, 29

subject: Mathematics
q: A circle passes through the points $(2,3)$ and $(4$, 5). If its centre lies on the line, $y-4 x+3=0$, then its radius is equal to
question: A circle passes through the points $(2,3)$ and $(4$, 5). If its centre lies on the line, $y-4 x+3=0$, then its radius is equal to
solution:
Equation of the line passing through the points $(2,3)$ and $(4,5)$ is
$$
y-3=\left(\frac{5-3}{4-2}\right) x-2 \Rightarrow x-y+1=0
$$
Equation of the perpendicular line passing through the midpoint $(3,4)$ is $x+y-7=0$
Lines (1) and (2) intersect at the center of the circle. So, the center of the circle is $(3,4)$ Therefore, the radius is
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\sqrt{\left(x_2-x_1\right)^2+\left(y_2-y_1\right)^2} \\
&=\sqrt{(2-3)^2+(3-4)^2}=\sqrt{2} \text { units. }
\end{aligned}
$$

q: Let the mirror image of a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>  in line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>g</mi><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>f</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>38</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is the radius of circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ______
question: Let the mirror image of a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>  in line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>g</mi><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>f</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>38</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is the radius of circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ______
solution:
Let the mirror image of a circle c1:x2+y2-2x-6y+α=0  in line y=x+1 be c2:5x2+5y2+10gx +10fy+38=0. If r is the radius of circle c2, then α+6r2 is equal to ______

Now Image of centre c1≡1,3 in x-y+1=0 is given by

x1-11=y1-3-1=-21-3+112+12

⇒x1=2,y1=2

So, centre of circle c2≡2,2

Now radius of circle c2 will be  4+4-385=25=r

Now radius of c12=radius of c22 as they are image to one another,

⇒12+32-α=25

⇒10-α=25⇒α=485

Now putting the value of α & r in α+6r2 we get,

α+6r2=485+125=12

subject: Mathematics
q: The common tangent to the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>24</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> also passes through the point:
question: The common tangent to the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>24</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> also passes through the point:
solution:
The centre and radius of the circle x2+y2+2gx+2fy+c=0 are respectively -g, -f and g2+f2-c.

Let, for the circle S1:x2+y2=4 centre C1:0, 0, r1=2 units.

And, for the circle S2:x2+y2+6x+8y-24=0 centre

C2-3, -4, r2=32+42+24=7 units.

The distance between the points x1, y1 and x2, y2 is x1-x22+y1-y22

Hence, the distance between the centres of the circles is C1C2=-3-02+-4-02=5 units.

Also, r1-r2=5 units.

Since, C1C2=r1-r2, hence both the circles touches each other internally.

Therefore, the common tangent is same as the radical axis of the two circles i.e. S1-S2=0

Thus, the common tangent is x2+y2-4-x2+y2+6x+8y-24=0

⇒  -6x-8y+20=0

⇒  3x+4y-10=0.

Clearly, the point 6,-2 lies on the common tangent.

Let the angle between the two radii (one to each circle) drawn from one of the intersection points of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><msqrt><mn>63</mn></msqrt><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>. If the length of common chord of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></math> equals _________.
question: Consider two circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>16</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>)</mo></math>. solution:

Now, from the above diagram, finding the area of  ΔOAP we get,

q: Let $C$ be the circle with centre $(0,0)$ and radius 3 units. The equation of the locus of the mid points of the chords of the circle $C$ that subtend an angle of $\frac{2 \pi}{3}$ at its centre is
question: Let $C$ be the circle with centre $(0,0)$ and radius 3 units. The equation of the locus of the mid points of the chords of the circle $C$ that subtend an angle of $\frac{2 \pi}{3}$ at its centre is
solution: $\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{h^2+k^2}}{3} \Rightarrow h^2+k^2=\frac{9}{4}$
